---
title: "Vignettes"
---

```{=html}
<style>
.vignette-grid {
  display: grid;
  grid-template-columns: repeat(2, 1fr);
  gap: 1rem;
  margin-bottom: 1.5rem;
}
@media (max-width: 768px) {
  .vignette-grid { grid-template-columns: 1fr; }
}
.vignette-card {
  border: 1px solid #dee2e6;
  border-left: 4px solid #2780e3;
  border-radius: 6px;
  padding: 1.1rem 1.4rem;
  background: #fff;
  transition: box-shadow 0.15s;
  display: flex;
  flex-direction: column;
}
.vignette-card:hover {
  box-shadow: 0 2px 12px rgba(0,0,0,0.08);
}
.vignette-lang {
  font-size: 0.72rem;
  font-weight: 600;
  text-transform: uppercase;
  letter-spacing: 0.06em;
  color: #6c757d;
  margin-bottom: 0.5rem;
}
.vignette-title {
  font-size: 1rem;
  font-weight: 600;
  margin-bottom: 0.5rem;
  line-height: 1.4;
}
.vignette-title a {
  color: #212529;
  text-decoration: none;
}
.vignette-title a:hover { color: #2780e3; }
.vignette-question {
  font-size: 0.85rem;
  color: #495057;
  font-style: italic;
  line-height: 1.5;
  flex: 1;
  margin-bottom: 0.75rem;
}
.vignette-packages {
  display: flex;
  flex-wrap: wrap;
  gap: 0.35rem;
  margin-top: auto;
}
.vignette-pkg {
  font-size: 0.75rem;
  font-family: monospace;
  background: #f1f3f5;
  color: #495057;
  border: 1px solid #dee2e6;
  border-radius: 3px;
  padding: 0.1rem 0.4rem;
}
.vignette-cite {
  font-size: 0.78rem;
  color: #6c757d;
  margin-bottom: 0.6rem;
}
.vignette-cite a {
  color: #2780e3;
  text-decoration: none;
}
.vignette-cite a:hover {
  text-decoration: underline;
}
.vignette-group-desc {
  color: #495057;
  margin-bottom: 1rem;
}
</style>
```

These vignettes show how to work with IRW data for common psychometric tasks.

::: {.callout-note}
Many of the vignettes below — including the dataset search, compute scripts,
analysis design, and writing — were produced largely by Claude (Anthropic),
working from a task specification and with human review. Individual pages
note this explicitly where it applies; treat the methodological choices and
interpretations accordingly, and check the underlying compute scripts
directly if you're relying on the numbers shown.
:::

## R

Each of these vignettes uses the [`irw` R package](https://itemresponsewarehouse.github.io/Rpkg/) to fetch data and walks through a complete analysis. They fall into four rough genres, grouped below.

### Tutorials

Introductions to a technique or tool, worked through on one example dataset.

<div class="vignette-grid">

<div class="vignette-card">
<div class="vignette-lang">R</div>
<div class="vignette-title"><a href="./imv.qmd">Calculating IMV Values</a></div>
<div class="vignette-question">How much does a 2PL improve on a 1PL for a given dataset? A short worked example computing cross-validated InterModel Vigorish (IMV) for one dataset, with a pointer to an interactive app for building intuition about what IMV values mean.</div>
<div class="vignette-cite">Key paper: <a href="https://doi.org/10.1007/s11336-024-09977-2" target="_blank">Domingue et al. (2025), Psychometrika</a></div>
<div class="vignette-cite">Also see: <a href="https://intermodelvigorish.github.io/shinysite/" target="_blank">the IMV Shiny app</a>, for building intuition about what different IMV values mean</div>
<div class="vignette-packages">
<span class="vignette-pkg">irw</span>
<span class="vignette-pkg">mirt</span>
<span class="vignette-pkg">imv</span>
</div>
</div>

<div class="vignette-card">
<div class="vignette-lang">R</div>
<div class="vignette-title"><a href="./diffsim.qmd">Simulating Item Difficulties</a></div>
<div class="vignette-question">How can I simulate item difficulties that reflect real-world distributions rather than idealized assumptions? Draws from a pooled empirical distribution built from 145+ IRW datasets via <code>irw_simu_diff()</code>, instead of assuming a normal or uniform shape.</div>
<div class="vignette-cite">Key paper: <a href="https://doi.org/10.31234/osf.io/jbhxy_v2" target="_blank">Zhang et al. (2025), PsyArXiv</a></div>
<div class="vignette-packages">
<span class="vignette-pkg">irw</span>
</div>
</div>

<div class="vignette-card">
<div class="vignette-lang">R</div>
<div class="vignette-title"><a href="./cfa.qmd">Conducting CFA with IRW Data</a></div>
<div class="vignette-question">How do I fit a multi-factor CFA model to an IRW personality dataset? Walks through a full lavaan workflow on a Big Five dataset — cleaning, reshaping to wide format, and building a multi-factor model directly from item-naming conventions — that generalizes to most other multi-scale IRW tables.</div>
<div class="vignette-packages">
<span class="vignette-pkg">irw</span>
<span class="vignette-pkg">lavaan</span>
<span class="vignette-pkg">psych</span>
</div>
</div>

</div>

### Assumption audits across IRW

Broad scans checking whether an assumption standard IRT models rely on actually holds, across hundreds of real IRW tables at once, rather than in a single example dataset.

<div class="vignette-grid">

<div class="vignette-card">
<div class="vignette-lang">R</div>
<div class="vignette-title"><a href="./dimensionality.qmd">Dimensionality Across IRW</a></div>
<div class="vignette-question">How often does the unidimensionality assumption behind standard IRT models actually hold up across real IRW instruments, and does it vary by construct type? Compares two exploratory diagnostics — the eigenvalue ratio and parallel analysis — across hundreds of tables and finds they disagree sharply on how often it holds.</div>
<div class="vignette-cite">Key paper: <a href="https://doi.org/10.1007/BF02289447" target="_blank">Horn (1965), Psychometrika</a> (parallel analysis)</div>
<div class="vignette-packages">
<span class="vignette-pkg">irw</span>
<span class="vignette-pkg">psych</span>
<span class="vignette-pkg">ggplot2</span>
</div>
</div>

<div class="vignette-card">
<div class="vignette-lang">R</div>
<div class="vignette-title"><a href="./local_dependence.qmd">Local Dependence Across IRW</a></div>
<div class="vignette-question">After fitting the standard unidimensional IRT model, how often are item pairs still residually correlated (local dependence / testlet effects), and does it concentrate in particular item formats? Flags pairs via Yen's Q3 statistic across ~800 tables and checks two known testlet structures as a sanity check.</div>
<div class="vignette-cite">Key paper: Yen (1984), Applied Psychological Measurement (Q3 statistic)</div>
<div class="vignette-packages">
<span class="vignette-pkg">irw</span>
<span class="vignette-pkg">mirt</span>
<span class="vignette-pkg">ggplot2</span>
</div>
</div>

<div class="vignette-card">
<div class="vignette-lang">R</div>
<div class="vignette-title"><a href="./response_style.qmd">Extreme and Midpoint Responding in Likert-Format IRW Data</a></div>
<div class="vignette-question">How prevalent are extreme (ERS) and midpoint (MRS) response styles in Likert-format IRW data, and do they distort substantive conclusions like factor loadings if left unmodeled? Screens IRW's Likert-scale tables and compares single-factor CFA loadings before and after a simple ipsatization-based style control.</div>
<div class="vignette-packages">
<span class="vignette-pkg">irw</span>
<span class="vignette-pkg">lavaan</span>
<span class="vignette-pkg">ggplot2</span>
</div>
</div>

<div class="vignette-card">
<div class="vignette-lang">R</div>
<div class="vignette-title"><a href="./gender_dif.qmd">Gender DIF Across IRW Datasets</a></div>
<div class="vignette-question">How prevalent is gender DIF across a diverse collection of real-world datasets, and do Mantel-Haenszel and logistic regression methods agree on which items are flagged? Runs both DIF families side by side on every IRW dataset with a gender/sex covariate.</div>
<div class="vignette-packages">
<span class="vignette-pkg">irw</span>
<span class="vignette-pkg">difR</span>
<span class="vignette-pkg">ggplot2</span>
</div>
</div>

</div>

### Replicating published findings on real IRW data

Takes a specific claim or theoretical result from a published paper and checks whether it holds up against real IRW data.

<div class="vignette-grid">

<div class="vignette-card">
<div class="vignette-lang">R</div>
<div class="vignette-title"><a href="./dutch_identity.qmd">The Dutch Identity</a></div>
<div class="vignette-question">What does Holland's (1990) Dutch Identity reveal about the structure of IRT models — and does it hold in real data? Six sections moving from raw manifest probabilities to a Rasch-model diagnostic and a test of Holland's conservation-of-complexity law.</div>
<div class="vignette-cite">Key paper: <a href="https://doi.org/10.1007/BF02294739" target="_blank">Holland (1990), Psychometrika</a></div>
<div class="vignette-cite">Also see: <a href="https://intermodelvigorish.github.io/shinysite/" target="_blank">the IMV Shiny app</a>, for the IMV values used in Section 6</div>
<div class="vignette-packages">
<span class="vignette-pkg">irw</span>
<span class="vignette-pkg">mirt</span>
<span class="vignette-pkg">imv</span>
<span class="vignette-pkg">ggplot2</span>
</div>
</div>

<div class="vignette-card">
<div class="vignette-lang">R</div>
<div class="vignette-title"><a href="./hf_reliability_paradox.qmd">The Reliability Paradox in Cognitive-Control Tasks</a></div>
<div class="vignette-question">Do congruent/incongruent difference scores in IRW cognitive-control tasks show the same reliability collapse Hedge, Powell & Sumner (2018) found in the lab? Tests four IRW tasks — Hearts &amp; Flowers, an alcohol Stroop, a stop-signal task, and three Self-Regulation Ontology tasks — for the same attenuation.</div>
<div class="vignette-cite">Key paper: <a href="https://doi.org/10.3758/s13428-017-0935-1" target="_blank">Hedge, Powell &amp; Sumner (2018), Behavior Research Methods</a></div>
<div class="vignette-packages">
<span class="vignette-pkg">irw</span>
<span class="vignette-pkg">data.table</span>
<span class="vignette-pkg">ggplot2</span>
</div>
</div>

</div>

### Cross-dataset analyses

Applied methods and novel analyses that use IRW's breadth to ask a research question that a single dataset couldn't answer on its own.

<div class="vignette-grid">

<div class="vignette-card">
<div class="vignette-lang">R</div>
<div class="vignette-title"><a href="./2pl_across_datasets.qmd">2PL Parameters Across Datasets</a></div>
<div class="vignette-question">What do discrimination parameters look like across many cognitive/educational datasets in the wild? Fits a 2PL to every eligible table and asks how consequential the Rasch model's a=1 constraint really is in practice.</div>
<div class="vignette-packages">
<span class="vignette-pkg">irw</span>
<span class="vignette-pkg">mirt</span>
<span class="vignette-pkg">ggplot2</span>
</div>
</div>

<div class="vignette-card">
<div class="vignette-lang">R</div>
<div class="vignette-title"><a href="./item_text_difficulty.qmd">Item Text and Difficulty</a></div>
<div class="vignette-question">Can simple linguistic features of item wording predict proportion correct across cognitive datasets? Extracts features like length, negation, and complexity from item text and tests how much of item difficulty they explain, separate from substantive content.</div>
<div class="vignette-packages">
<span class="vignette-pkg">irw</span>
<span class="vignette-pkg">ggplot2</span>
</div>
</div>

<div class="vignette-card">
<div class="vignette-lang">R</div>
<div class="vignette-title"><a href="./rt_imv.qmd">Response Time and Accuracy: An IMV Analysis</a></div>
<div class="vignette-question">How much does knowing how fast a person responded improve predictions of whether they answered correctly, over and above IRT ability estimates? Uses cross-validated IMV to isolate the predictive value response time adds beyond ability alone.</div>
<div class="vignette-cite">Key paper: <a href="https://doi.org/10.3102/10769986221099115" target="_blank">Domingue et al. (2022), Journal of Educational and Behavioral Statistics</a></div>
<div class="vignette-cite">Also see: <a href="https://intermodelvigorish.github.io/shinysite/" target="_blank">the IMV Shiny app</a>, for building intuition about what different IMV values mean</div>
<div class="vignette-packages">
<span class="vignette-pkg">irw</span>
<span class="vignette-pkg">mirt</span>
<span class="vignette-pkg">imv</span>
<span class="vignette-pkg">splines</span>
</div>
</div>

<div class="vignette-card">
<div class="vignette-lang">R</div>
<div class="vignette-title"><a href="./il_hte.qmd">Item-Level Heterogeneous Treatment Effects</a></div>
<div class="vignette-question">When do items in an RCT outcome measure respond differently to treatment, and what does ignoring this do to treatment-by-covariate interaction estimates? Shows that item-level variation in treatment response can bias estimated interactions even when the average treatment effect looks fine.</div>
<div class="vignette-cite">Key paper: <a href="https://doi.org/10.1002/pam.70025" target="_blank">Gilbert et al. (2025), Journal of Policy Analysis and Management</a></div>
<div class="vignette-packages">
<span class="vignette-pkg">irw</span>
<span class="vignette-pkg">lme4</span>
</div>
</div>

</div>

## Python

These vignettes use the [`irw` Python package](https://github.com/itemresponsewarehouse/Python-pkg) to fetch data.

<div class="vignette-grid">

<div class="vignette-card">
<div class="vignette-lang">Python</div>
<div class="vignette-title"><a href="./irt_python.qmd">IRT Models in Python</a></div>
<div class="vignette-question">How do I fetch IRW data in Python and fit 1PL, 2PL, 3PL, and graded response models?</div>
<div class="vignette-packages">
<span class="vignette-pkg">irw</span>
<span class="vignette-pkg">mirt</span>
<span class="vignette-pkg">girth</span>
</div>
</div>

</div>
